questions: Does the new extractTarGz function correctly replace the execSync tar call without breaking the error contract?
Does the implementation use only node:* built-in modules with zero third-party dependencies?
Do all 5 extraction test cases pass, including the colon-in-path test that validates the original Windows GNU tar failure mode?
Does npm run build and npm test pass with no regressions to existing tests?
Is the execSync import retained in perform.ts only for the copyDirRecursive EXDEV fallback (not for tar)?

answers.answer: Yes. The extractTarGz function is called at perform.ts:125 inside the same try/catch block (lines 124-129). It throws on error, which is caught and converted to { success: false, error: 'Extraction failed: ...' } — identical error contract to the prior execSync call.

Yes. extract.ts imports only node:zlib, node:fs, and node:path. extract.test.ts imports only node:test, node:assert, node:fs, node:path, node:os, and node:zlib. No third-party modules are used anywhere.
evidence: grep '^import' src/update/extract.ts shows only node:zlib, node:fs, node:path
grep '^import' src/update/extract.test.ts shows only node:* modules

answers.answer: Yes. All 5 tests pass: (1) representative CI tarball extraction with content verification, (2) colon-in-path extraction succeeds, (3) corrupt tarball throws, (4) empty archive is handled gracefully, (5) PAX headers are skipped and subsequent file is extracted correctly.
evidence: node --test dist/update/extract.test.js output: 5 tests, 5 pass, 0 fail

answers.answer: Yes. npm run build exits 0. npm test runs all 56 tests (51 existing + 5 new) with 0 failures. node dist/index.js --version outputs '1.3.4'.
evidence: npm test output: tests 56, pass 56, fail 0

answers.answer: Yes. grep -n 'execSync' src/update/perform.ts shows only two matches: line 1 (import) and line 34 (copyDirRecursive function). No tar invocation remains.
evidence: grep -n execSync src/update/perform.ts: line 1 import, line 34 copyDirRecursive

statement: Implementation complete. The execSync tar call at perform.ts:124 has been replaced with an in-process extractTarGz function using only Node.js built-in modules (node:zlib, node:fs, node:path). This eliminates the dependency on any external tar binary, resolving the Windows GNU tar failure where drive-letter colons are interpreted as remote-host syntax. The error contract is preserved exactly. Five test cases cover representative extraction, colon-in-path (original failure mode), corrupt input, empty archive, and PAX header handling. All 56 tests pass, the build succeeds, and no third-party dependencies were added.
